[Roy Romanow](/wiki/Roy_Romanow "Roy Romanow")  [New Democratic](/wiki/Saskatchewan_New_Democratic_Party "Saskatchewan New Democratic Party") | **Premier after election**   [Roy Romanow](/wiki/Roy_Romanow "Roy Romanow")  [New Democratic](/wiki/Saskatchewan_New_Democratic_Party "Saskatchewan New Democratic Party") | | |

was held on September 16, 1999 to elect members of the [24th Legislative Assembly of Saskatchewan](/wiki/24th_Legislative_Assembly_of_Saskatchewan "24th Legislative Assembly of Saskatchewan"). The new Saskatchewan Party took more votes than any other party but the NDP took more seats, taking half the seats in the Saskatchewan Legislature. The NDP formed a coalition with four elected Liberal Party MLAs to hold majority government.
\* Party did not nominate candidates in previous election.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Canadian provincial election

1999 Saskatchewan general election

| |  | First party | Second party | Third party | | --- | --- | --- | --- | |  |  |  | **LIB** | | Leader | [Roy Romanow](/wiki/Roy_Romanow "Roy Romanow") | [Elwin Hermanson](/wiki/Elwin_Hermanson "Elwin Hermanson") | [Jim Melenchuk](/wiki/Jim_Melenchuk "Jim Melenchuk") | | Party | [New Democratic](/wiki/Saskatchewan_New_Democratic_Party "Saskatchewan New Democratic Party") | [Saskatchewan](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Party "Saskatchewan Party") | [Liberal](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Progress_Party "Saskatchewan Progress Party") | | Leader since | [November 7, 1987](/wiki/Saskatchewan_New_Democratic_Party/Co-operative_Commonwealth_Federation_leadership_elections#1987_New_Democratic_Party_leadership_convention "Saskatchewan New Democratic Party/Co-operative Commonwealth Federation leadership elections") | [April 20, 1998](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Party_leadership_elections#1998_leadership_convention "Saskatchewan Party leadership elections") | [November 24, 1996](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Liberal_Party_leadership_elections#Liberal_leadership_convention,_1996 "Saskatchewan Liberal Party leadership elections") | | Leader's seat | [Saskatoon Riversdale](/wiki/Saskatoon_Riversdale "Saskatoon Riversdale") | [Rosetown-Elrose](/wiki/Rosetown-Elrose "Rosetown-Elrose") | [Saskatoon Northwest](/wiki/Saskatoon_Northwest "Saskatoon Northwest") | | Last election | 42 | *pre-creation* | 11 | | Seats before | 43 | 10 | 5 | | Seats won | **29** | 25 | 4 | | Seat change | Decrease14 | Increase15 | Decrease1 | | Popular vote | 157,046 | **160,603** | 81,694 | | Percentage | 38.73% | **39.61%** | 20.15% | | Swing | Decrease8.48[pp](/wiki/Percentage_point "Percentage point") | Increase39.61 | Decrease14.55pp | | |
| --- | |
| ---  |  |  | | --- | --- | | **Premier before election**  [Roy Romanow](/wiki/Roy_Romanow "Roy Romanow")  [New Democratic](/wiki/Saskatchewan_New_Democratic_Party "Saskatchewan New Democratic Party") | **Premier after election**   [Roy Romanow](/wiki/Roy_Romanow "Roy Romanow")  [New Democratic](/wiki/Saskatchewan_New_Democratic_Party "Saskatchewan New Democratic Party") | | |

The **1999 Saskatchewan general election** was held on September 16, 1999 to elect members of the [24th Legislative Assembly of Saskatchewan](/wiki/24th_Legislative_Assembly_of_Saskatchewan "24th Legislative Assembly of Saskatchewan"). The new Saskatchewan Party took more votes than any other party but the NDP took more seats, taking half the seats in the Saskatchewan Legislature. The NDP formed a coalition with four elected Liberal Party MLAs to hold majority government.

Polls during the campaign indicated strong levels of support for the [New Democratic Party](/wiki/New_Democratic_Party_of_Saskatchewan "New Democratic Party of Saskatchewan") government.[[2]](#cite_note-poll-2)[[3]](#cite_note-polls-3) However, facing the fallout of a poor crop growing season and a scandal involving the [Crown Corporation](/wiki/State-owned_enterprise "State-owned enterprise") [electric utility](/wiki/Electricity_sector_in_Canada#Saskatchewan "Electricity sector in Canada") [SaskPower](/wiki/SaskPower "SaskPower") (Channel Lake), the New Democrat government of [Premier](/wiki/Premier_of_Saskatchewan "Premier of Saskatchewan") [Roy Romanow](/wiki/Roy_Romanow "Roy Romanow") – challenged by the newly created [Saskatchewan Party](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Party "Saskatchewan Party") – lost a significant share of the popular vote; winning exactly half of the fifty eight seats in the legislature.

The right-wing Saskatchewan Party was created during the [sitting of the 23rd Assembly](/wiki/23rd_Saskatchewan_Legislature "23rd Saskatchewan Legislature") when much of the [Progressive Conservative](/wiki/Progressive_Conservative_Party_of_Saskatchewan "Progressive Conservative Party of Saskatchewan") caucus joined forces with [conservative](/wiki/Conservative_liberalism "Conservative liberalism") [Liberals](/wiki/Liberal_Party_of_Saskatchewan "Liberal Party of Saskatchewan") who were unhappy with the leadership of [Jim Melenchuk](/wiki/Jim_Melenchuk "Jim Melenchuk").

The new party was led by [Elwin Hermanson](/wiki/Elwin_Hermanson "Elwin Hermanson"), a former [Reform Party](/wiki/Reform_Party_of_Canada "Reform Party of Canada") federal [Member of Parliament](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_Canada "House of Commons of Canada"). In this election, it won 39.61% of the popular vote – slightly more than the NDP's 38.73%. However, this was only enough for 25 seats, five short of making Hermanson premier. This was mainly because it was almost nonexistent in the province's more urban areas; it was completely shut out in [Regina](/wiki/Regina,_Saskatchewan "Regina, Saskatchewan") and won only one seat in [Saskatoon](/wiki/Saskatoon "Saskatoon").

The NDP was able to continue to govern with the support of some Liberal [Members of the Legislative Assembly](/wiki/Member_of_the_Legislative_Assembly "Member of the Legislative Assembly") (MLAs).

Some NDP members unhappy with the government of Roy Romanow left to form the [New Green Alliance](/wiki/Green_Party_of_Saskatchewan "Green Party of Saskatchewan"), an environmentalist party. This party won about 1% of the popular vote, and no seats in the legislature.

What remained of the Progressive Conservatives fielded 14 [paper candidates](/wiki/Paper_candidate "Paper candidate") – all in NDP strongholds – in order to preserve their status as a registered political party. The Tories did not actively campaign and won only a few votes.

To date, this is the most recent general election to return MLAs who were members of neither the NDP nor the Saskatchewan Party.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/b/be/Sleg99.PNG)](/wiki/File:Sleg99.PNG)

Seating Plan After the Election

### 8 closest ridings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1999_Saskatchewan_general_election&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 8 closest ridings")]

1. **[Wood River](/wiki/Wood_River_(electoral_district) "Wood River (electoral district)")**: [Yogi Huyghebaert](/wiki/Yogi_Huyghebaert "Yogi Huyghebaert") (SK Party) def. Glen McPherson (Lib) by 7 votes1
2. **[Saskatoon Southeast](/wiki/Saskatoon_Southeast "Saskatoon Southeast")**: [Pat Lorje](/wiki/Pat_Lorje "Pat Lorje") (NDP) def. Grant Karwacki (Lib) by 38 votes
3. **[Regina Wascana Plains](/wiki/Regina_Wascana_Plains "Regina Wascana Plains")**: [Doreen Hamilton](/wiki/Doreen_Hamilton "Doreen Hamilton") (NDP) def. Dan Thibault (SK Party) by 119 votes
4. **[Saskatoon Northwest](/wiki/Saskatoon_Northwest "Saskatoon Northwest")**: [Jim Melenchuk](/wiki/Jim_Melenchuk "Jim Melenchuk") (Lib) def. Grant Whitmore (NDP) by 127 votes
5. **[Saskatchewan Rivers](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Rivers "Saskatchewan Rivers")**: Daryl Wiberg (SK Party) def. Jack Langford (NDP) by 156 votes
6. **[Shellbrook-Spiritwood](/wiki/Shellbrook-Spiritwood "Shellbrook-Spiritwood")**: [Denis Allchurch](/wiki/Denis_Allchurch "Denis Allchurch") (SK Party) def. Lloyd Johnson (NDP) by 301 votes
7. **[Yorkton](/wiki/Yorkton_(provincial_electoral_district) "Yorkton (provincial electoral district)")**: [Clay Serby](/wiki/Clay_Serby "Clay Serby") (NDP) def. Lorne Gogal (SK Party) by 306 votes
8. **[Meadow Lake](/wiki/Meadow_Lake_(provincial_electoral_district) "Meadow Lake (provincial electoral district)")**: [Maynard Sonntag](/wiki/Maynard_Sonntag "Maynard Sonntag") (NDP) def. Bob Young (SK Party) by 323 votes

**Notes:** 1 see below under "Wood River controversy"

## Wood River controversy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1999_Saskatchewan_general_election&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Wood River controversy")]

The Wood River electoral district in the wake of the 1999 general election endured a nine-month crisis where it went without representation.
On election night returns came back in favour of Saskatchewan Party candidate [Yogi Huyghebaert](/wiki/Yogi_Huyghebaert "Yogi Huyghebaert") who defeated incumbent Glen McPherson by just seven votes in unofficial returns.[[10]](#cite_note-earlyreturns-10) The close election results were challenged in the courts.

After five months a judicial decision came down and the results were certified on January 27, 2000. [Saskatchewan Liberal Party](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Liberal_Party "Saskatchewan Liberal Party") incumbent [Glen McPherson](/wiki/Glen_McPherson_(Saskatchewan_politician) "Glen McPherson (Saskatchewan politician)") was declared by a judge the winner by a single vote defeating [Yogi Huyghebaert](/wiki/Yogi_Huyghebaert "Yogi Huyghebaert") from the [Saskatchewan Party](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Party "Saskatchewan Party").[[11]](#cite_note-declare-11) The Saskatchewan Party decided to challenge the judicial decision, and it was overturned and dissolved based on irregularities in the absentee ballots.

The seat was dissolved and a by-election was called by Premier [Roy Romanow](/wiki/Roy_Romanow "Roy Romanow") on May 29, 2000.[[12]](#cite_note-called-12) McPherson did not run in the subsequent by-election, choosing to reject the NDP-Liberal coalition. His candidacy for the Liberal party was replaced by Gerry Ruehs.[[13]](#cite_note-replace-13) Huyghebaert ended up winning the by-election.
